mission.title: Install and Configure Connect Rpc on Kubernetes
mission.description: Connect is a slim library for building browser and gRPC-compatible HTTP APIs using Protocol Buffers. You would install it to create efficient APIs that work seamlessly across different platforms and protocols.

steps.title: Clone the repository
steps.description:
Clone the Connect repository to your local machine using the following command:
```bash
git clone https://github.com/connectrpc/connect-go.git
cd connect-go
```

steps.title: Build the Connect library
steps.description:
Build the Connect library by running the following command in the project directory:
```bash
go build -o connect ./...
```

steps.title: Deploy the Connect service
steps.description:
Create a Kubernetes deployment for the Connect service. Save the following YAML to a file named `connect-deployment.yaml`:
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: connect
  namespace: connect
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: connect
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: connect
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: connect
        image: your-docker-registry/connect:1.19.1
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
``` 
Then apply it with:
```bash
kubectl apply -f connect-deployment.yaml --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Expose the Connect service
steps.description:
Create a service to expose the Connect deployment. Save the following YAML to a file named `connect-service.yaml`:
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: connect
  namespace: connect
spec:
  type: NodePort
  ports:
    - port: 8080
      targetPort: 8080
      nodePort: 30000
  selector:
    app: connect
```
Then apply it with:
```bash
kubectl apply -f connect-service.yaml
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Connect pods are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n connect
```
You should see the Connect pod in a Running state. You can also port-forward to access the service:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/connect 8080:8080 -n connect
```

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
Configure resource limits for the Connect deployment. Edit the `connect-deployment.yaml` file to include resource limits:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    memory: "512Mi"
    cpu: "500m"
  requests:
    memory: "256Mi"
    cpu: "250m"
```
Then reapply the deployment:
```bash
kubectl apply -f connect-deployment.yaml -n connect
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Connect service
uninstall.description:
Delete the Connect service with the following command:
```bash
kubectl delete -f connect-service.yaml -n connect
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Connect deployment
uninstall.description:
Delete the Connect deployment with the following command:
```bash
kubectl delete -f connect-deployment.yaml -n connect
```

uninstall.title: Clean up the namespace
uninstall.description:
Remove the Connect namespace if it is no longer needed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace connect
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current deployment configuration:
```bash
kubectl get deployment connect -n connect -o yaml > connect-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Connect library
upgrade.description:
Pull the latest changes from the repository and build the new version:
```bash
git pull origin main
go build -o connect ./...
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade the deployment
upgrade.description:
Update the deployment with the new image version:
```bash
kubectl set image deployment/connect connect=your-docker-registry/connect:1.19.1 -n connect
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check if the Connect pods are running the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n connect
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods not starting
troubleshooting.description:
If the Connect pods are not starting, check the pod logs:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n connect
```
Look for any error messages indicating what might be wrong.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Connect service, ensure the service is running:
```bash
kubectl get svc -n connect
```
Check if the correct ports are exposed and the service type is set to NodePort.

troubleshooting.title: Deployment not updated
troubleshooting.description:
If the deployment does not reflect the new changes, check the rollout status:
```bash
kubectl rollout status deployment/connect -n connect
```
If it is stuck, you may need to restart the deployment:
```bash
kubectl rollout restart deployment/connect -n connect
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Connect will have the deployment running with the specified replicas and the service accessible via the defined NodePort. You can verify the functionality by making API calls to the service.
codeSnippets:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: connect
  namespace: connect
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: connect
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: connect
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: connect
        image: your-docker-registry/connect:1.19.1
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: connect
  namespace: connect
spec:
  type: NodePort
  ports:
    - port: 8080
      targetPort: 8080
      nodePort: 30000
  selector:
    app: connect

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and the Go programming language installed.
